tasks.prompt: Find representative public Moby-Dick source categories for the main text, alternate text, and contributor metadata. Use the pinned public corpus copy and summarize only public evidence categories/counts.
Answer from the public Moby-Dick corpus: what does the opening narrator tell the reader about himself? Include public citation paths only.

Create or update a disposable public synthesis at synthesis/public-vault/moby-dick/opening-narrator.md titled Moby-Dick Opening Narrator Notes. Use source_refs sources/gitenberg/moby-dick/2701-0.md and sources/gitenberg/moby-dick/book.md. Body facts should explain that the corpus is public literary text and that the narrator frames the opening in first person.
body_facts: The public corpus contains the Moby-Dick text in multiple public-domain source forms.
The opening narrator frames the story in first person and gives the famous self-identifying opening.
The non-technical lane checks literary source handling without private snippets, local paths, or runner internals in committed reports.

tasks.prompt: Package public evidence and freshness for the Moby-Dick main text. Use sources/gitenberg/moby-dick/2701-0.md as the document ref when useful.
Find decision-like public evidence about edition or metadata choices in the Moby-Dick corpus. If there is no formal ADR-style decision record, say so and answer from public corpus evidence.
Before creating any new note, check public Moby-Dick corpus files for duplicate main-text forms and projection freshness. Report whether a new synthesis would duplicate existing public sources.
Compare the public Moby-Dick corpus boundaries between the main UTF-8 text, alternate text, and book source. Use public citations and do not write a synthesis.
Find the public Moby-Dick authority files for the main text, old text, and book source, then summarize which file a reader should inspect first. Prefer the source discovery surface.
